requirements.location: code/src/griffe/dataclasses.py — class Class (currently ends at line ~1408)
requirements.description:
The `Class` dataclass is missing a `mro()` method. Tests call `cls.mro()` expecting a
      list of resolved `Class` objects representing the Method Resolution Order (C3 linearization)
      of the class, excluding the class itself and built-in base types (e.g., `object`, `str`,
      dynamic/unresolvable bases). Currently `Class` has no such method, causing
      `AttributeError: 'Class' object has no attribute 'mro'` in all MRO-related tests.
requirements.contract:
- `Class.mro() -> list[Class | Alias]`: Returns the C3-linearized MRO of the class,
        excluding the class itself. Only includes bases that can be resolved within the loaded
        modules collection. Unresolvable bases (e.g., built-ins, dynamically created types,
        bases from unloaded modules) are silently skipped from the MRO but do not cause an error.
      - If the MRO is uncomputable due to inconsistent ordering (e.g., duplicate base, cyclic
        inheritance), `mro()` must raise `ValueError` with a message matching "Cannot compute
        C3 linearization".
      - When no resolvable bases exist (e.g., class inherits only from dynamic/built-in types),
        `mro()` returns an empty list `[]`.
      - The returned list contains objects whose `.path` attribute is the fully-qualified dotted
        path (e.g., `"module.ClassName"`), enabling MRO path comparisons as done in tests via
        `[base.path for base in cls.mro()]`.
      - Class resolution should use the modules collection accessible through the object's module
        hierarchy.

requirements.location: code/src/griffe/dataclasses.py — class Class (currently ends at line ~1408)
requirements.description:
The `Class` dataclass is missing a `resolved_bases` property. Tests access
      `cls.resolved_bases` expecting a list of resolved `Class`/`Alias` objects for each
      declared base class that can be found in the modules collection. Currently causes
      `AttributeError: 'Class' object has no attribute 'resolved_bases'`.
requirements.contract:
- `Class.resolved_bases -> list[Class | Alias]`: A property that returns a list of
        resolved object references for each base class declared in `self.bases`.
      - Only includes bases that can be successfully resolved against the modules collection.
        Bases that cannot be resolved (e.g., built-ins, dynamic expressions, unloaded modules)
        are silently omitted from the result.
      - The result preserves declaration order.
      - An actual `Class` object (or its `Alias`) appears in `resolved_bases` when that class
        was loaded into the modules collection, enabling identity comparisons like
        `classa in classb.resolved_bases`.

requirements.location: code/src/griffe/dataclasses.py — class Class (currently ends at line ~1408)
requirements.description:
The `Class` dataclass is missing `inherited_members` and `all_members` properties.
      Tests access `cls.inherited_members` expecting members inherited from parent classes
      (excluding own-declared and overridden members), and `cls.all_members` expecting the
      union of own and inherited members. Currently causes
      `AttributeError: 'Class' object has no attribute 'inherited_members'`.
requirements.contract:
- `Class.inherited_members -> dict[str, Object | Alias]`: A property that returns a
        dictionary of members inherited from ancestor classes via the MRO. A member is
        "inherited" if it exists in at least one ancestor class but is NOT defined directly
        in `self.members`. If a member is overridden/redefined in `self.members`, it must
        NOT appear in `inherited_members`. Members are collected by walking the MRO in order;
        the first ancestor that defines a member provides that member.
      - `Class.all_members -> dict[str, Object | Alias]`: A property that returns the union
        of `self.members` and `self.inherited_members`. Own members take precedence over
        inherited ones (i.e., if a name exists in both, the own member is returned).
      - If `mro()` cannot be computed (raises `ValueError`) or contains unresolvable classes,
        the method gracefully returns only what it can resolve without propagating exceptions.

requirements.description:
`_handle_pth_file` currently skips any line containing a semicolon (`";" not in line`
      on line 341). The test `test_pth_file_handling_with_semi_colon` provides a .pth file
      where a single line contains semicolon-separated entries (e.g.,
      `"import thing; import\tthing; /doesnotexist; tests"`). The expected behavior is that
      such a line is split by semicolons and each part is evaluated independently, yielding
      `[Path("tests")]`. Currently the entire line is discarded, so the function returns `[]`
      instead.
requirements.contract:
- When a line in a .pth file contains one or more semicolons, the line must be split
        on semicolons and each resulting part must be stripped and evaluated independently
        using the same rules as a standalone line:
          * Parts matching the `import ...` pattern are handled as editable-module imports.
          * Parts that are empty or begin with `#` are skipped.
          * Parts that correspond to existing paths on the filesystem are added to the
            result as `Path` objects.
          * Parts that do not exist on the filesystem are silently skipped.
      - The overall return value is `list[Path]` as before; the change is purely in how
        semicolon-containing lines are tokenized before evaluation.

requirements.description:
The `check()` function calls `_get_latest_tag(package)` which internally calls
      `subprocess.check_output(["git", "describe", "--tags", "--abbrev=0"], ...)`.
      When the given path is not a git repository (or has no tags), this raises
      `subprocess.CalledProcessError`. The test `test_git_failures` passes a plain
      temporary directory (not a git repo) and expects `check()` to return `2`,
      but currently the `CalledProcessError` propagates uncaught, causing the test to fail.
requirements.contract:
- The `check()` function must catch git-related failures (such as
        `subprocess.CalledProcessError` and `OSError`) that arise from
        `_get_latest_tag()` and `_get_repo_root()` when the provided path is not a valid
        git repository or has no tags.
      - On any such git failure, `check()` must return the integer `2` (distinct from the
        existing return codes `0` for success and `1` for API breakages or extension errors).
      - The function signature must remain unchanged. No other behavior should be affected.
